ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has no specific concept for Rajma (North Indian kidney bean curry). Nearest parent is a generic cooked kidney bean dish concept. A dedicated concept for Indian spiced kidney bean curry is recommended for future FoodOn extension.
ontology.foodex2_description: Cooked kidney bean curry, North Indian style (closest parent match; no exact FoodEx2 code exists for Rajma)
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A03SM used as closest parent for cooked legume-based curry. No exact code exists for Rajma specifically.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for Rajma or North Indian kidney bean curry as of April 2026.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of Rajma is approximately 200 g (one bowl), which is the typical portion served as a main course alongside steamed basmati rice in Indian home cooking and restaurants.

nutrient_source.title: Nutritive Value of Indian Foods — cooked kidney bean composition data and standard rajma recipe derivation
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research — National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values derived from ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods cooked kidney bean data, adjusted for standard rajma recipe composition including onion-tomato gravy and spices. USDA FoodData Central (kidney beans, cooked, boiled) used as secondary cross-reference for macro and micronutrient validation. Values represent the complete cooked dish per 100 g.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values — kidney beans (red, boiled)
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI value of 29 sourced from University of Sydney GI Database for red kidney beans (boiled). Rajma is a cooked kidney bean curry; the intact whole legume structure is preserved through pressure cooking, which maintains the low GI characteristic of kidney beans. GL per serving calculated as (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (29 × 41.0 g) / 100 ≈ 11.9. Confidence 0.9.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content 268 mg per 100 g (536 mg per 200 g bowl); partially offset by high potassium (318 mg/100 g) and magnesium (48 mg/100 g)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should be mindful of the moderate sodium content (~536 mg per bowl); however, the high potassium-to-sodium ratio and magnesium content from kidney beans support vascular health and may partially mitigate blood pressure elevation when consumed as part of a low-sodium diet.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low glycemic index (~29) and high dietary fibre (6.8 g/100 g) with resistant starch from intact kidney bean structure
type2_diabetes.guidance: Rajma is highly suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to its very low GI of approximately 29, a glycemic load of ~12 per bowl, and 6.8 g dietary fibre per 100 g, all of which contribute to slow glucose absorption and improved postprandial glycaemic control.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Zero cholesterol, very low saturated fat (0.4 g/100 g), high dietary fibre, and high folate (88 mcg DFE/100 g)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Rajma is cardioprotective: it contains no cholesterol, minimal saturated fat (0.4 g/100 g), and high soluble fibre that supports LDL reduction; the high folate content (88 mcg DFE/100 g) also supports homocysteine metabolism, reducing a key cardiovascular risk factor.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High folate content (88 mcg DFE/100 g) supports cell repair and DNA synthesis relevant to immune function
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Kidney beans in Rajma provide one of the highest folate concentrations among plant foods (88 mcg DFE/100 g), which supports cell repair and DNA synthesis important for immune regulation in rheumatoid arthritis management.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Rajma is a beloved North Indian and Punjabi comfort dish consisting of plump, pressure-cooked red kidney beans simmered in a thick, flavourful onion-tomato gravy spiced with cumin, coriander, garam masala, and ginger-garlic paste. The curry has a robust, hearty texture and a deep, earthy flavour profile that distinguishes it from lighter lentil-based dals. It is classically paired with plain steamed basmati rice in the iconic Rajma-Chawal combination, one of the most satisfying and nutritionally complete meals in Indian home cooking. Nutritionally, Rajma is exceptional: it provides 7.8 g protein, 6.8 g dietary fibre, and 88 mcg DFE folate per 100 g, with a very low glycemic index of approximately 29, making it outstanding for blood sugar management. It is naturally vegan, gluten-free, dairy-free, and halal. The high potassium (318 mg/100 g) and magnesium (48 mg/100 g) content support cardiovascular and blood pressure health. Rajma is widely consumed across India, particularly in Punjab and Jammu, and is increasingly popular in Indian diaspora communities globally, including in Singapore, Malaysia, and the United Kingdom.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values derived from ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods, the primary approved source for Indian dishes, cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central for cooked kidney beans. GI sourced from University of Sydney GI Database for red kidney beans (boiled). All core macros and key micronutrients are populated with high-confidence values. The dish is a canonical, well-documented North Indian preparation with stable composition.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for the complete cooked rajma dish is not separately reported in ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods or USDA FoodData Central kidney bean entries as of April 2026. water_g set to -1: moisture content for the complete cooked rajma dish is not reported in the primary source. trans_fat_g is confirmed 0.0 per ICMR-NIN source (no hydrogenated fats used in traditional preparation). cholesterol_mg is confirmed 0.0 — rajma is a plant-based dish containing no animal products; naturally zero cholesterol. vitamin_b12_mcg is confirmed 0.0 — rajma is fully plant-based; vitamin B12 is naturally absent in plant foods. vitamin_d_mcg is confirmed 0.0 — no significant vitamin D source in this dish; confirmed negligible per ICMR-NIN and USDA references. GI value of 29 is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database entry for red kidney beans (boiled); the intact legume structure is preserved in pressure-cooked rajma, supporting applicability of this GI value. Sodium of 268 mg/100 g reflects standard home-cooking salt addition; restaurant preparations may be higher.
